section.title: Helm Chart Description for Wind River Deployment
section.p: Customize the Helm chart using the Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_<release>/helmchart/jcnr/values.yaml file. We provide a copy of the default values.yaml in .
contains a description of the configurable attributes in values.yaml for a Wind River deployment.

table.title: Helm Chart Description for Wind River Deployment

row.entry: Set to true to install syslog-ng.

Defines the Docker registry for the Cloud-Native Router container images. The default value is enterprise-hub.juniper.net. The images provided in the tarball are tagged with the default registry name. If you choose to host the container images to a private registry, replace the default value with your registry URL.

(Optional) Defines the repository path for the Cloud-Native Router container images. This is a global key that takes precedence over the repository paths under the common section. Default is jcnr-container-prod/.
(Optional) Defines the Docker registry authentication credentials. You can configure credentials to either the Juniper Networks enterprise-hub.juniper.net registry or your private registry.
Base64 representation of your Docker registry credentials. See for more information.
Name of the secret object that will be created.
Deploys contrail-tools container that provides a centralized location for all vRouter tools and CLI commands.
Set to true to install the contrail-tools container.
Defines repository paths and tags for the various Cloud-Native Router container images. Use default unless using a private registry.
Defines the repository path. The default value is jcnr-container-prod/. The global repository key takes precedence if defined.
Defines the image tag. The default value is configured to the appropriate tag number for the Cloud-Native Router release version.

entry.p: Set to true to enable Cloud-Native Router Readiness preflight and postflight checks during installation. Comment this out or set to false to disable Cloud-Native Router Readiness preflight and postflight checks.
Preflight checks verify that your infrastructure can support JCNR. Preflight checks take place before Cloud-Native Router is installed.
Postflight checks verify that your Cloud-Native Router installation is working properly. Postflight checks take place after Cloud-Native Router is installed.
See .

row.entry: (Optional) Indicates the number of replicas for cRPD. Default is 1. The value for this key must be specified for multi-node clusters. The value is equal to the number of nodes running JCNR.

entry.p: Provide a list of interfaces to be bound to DPDK. You can also provide subnets instead of interface names. If both the interface name and the subnet are specified, then the interface name takes precedence over subnet/gateway combination. The subnet/gateway combination is useful when the interface names vary in a multi-node cluster.
For example:
This attribute and all of its child attributes are only applicable when running with non-pre-bound SR-IOV interfaces.
Comment out these attributes when running with pre-bound SR-IOV interfaces.

row.entry:
An alternative mode of input to interface names. For example: - subnet: 10.40.1.0/24 gateway: 10.40.1.1 ddp: "off"
The subnet option is applicable only for L3 interfaces. With the subnet mode of input, interfaces are auto-detected in each subnet. Specify either subnet/gateway or the interface name. Do not configure both. The subnet/gateway form of input is particularly helpful in environments where the interface names vary in a multi-node cluster.

entry.p: (Optional) Indicates the interface-level Dynamic Device Personalization (DDP) configuration. DDP provides datapath optimization at the NIC for traffic like GTPU, SCTP, etc. See for more details.
Options include auto, on, or off. Default is off.

note.p: The interface level ddp takes precedence over the global ddp configuration.

entry.p: Specifies the QoS scheduler profile applicable to this interface. See the qosSchedulerProfiles section.
If you don't specify a profile, then the QoS scheduler is disabled for this interface, which means that packets are scheduled with no regard to traffic class.

row.entry:
Defines the log severity. Available value options are: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, and ERR.
Leave it set to the default INFO unless instructed to change it by Juniper Networks support.

entry.p: The defined directory stores various JCNR-related descriptive logs such as contrail-vrouter-agent.log, contrail-vrouter-dpdk.log, etc. Default is /var/log/jcnr/.
Indicates the absolute path to the file that stores syslog-ng generated notifications in JSON format. Default is /var/log/jcnr/jcnr_notifications.json.
Indicates the core_pattern for the core file. If left blank, then Cloud-Native Router pods will not overwrite the default pattern on the host.

note.p: Set the core_pattern on the host before deploying JCNR. You can change the value in /etc/sysctl.conf. For example, kernel.core_pattern=/var/crash/core_%e_%p_%i_%s_%h_%t.gz

row.entry: Indicates the path to the core file. Default is /var/crash.

entry.p: (Optional) Defines labels on nodes to determine where to place the vRouter pods.
By default the vRouter pods are deployed to all nodes of a cluster.
In the example below, the node affinity label is defined as key1=jcnr. You must apply this label to each node where Cloud-Native Router is to be deployed:
entry.codeblock: nodeAffinity: - key: key1 operator: In values: - jcnr

note.p: This key is a global setting.

row.entry: Key-value pair that represents a node label that must be matched to apply the node affinity.
Defines the relationship between the node label and the set of values in the matchExpression parameters in the pod specification. This value can be In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Lt, or Gt.

entry.p: (Optional) Defines tolerations to allow JCNR pods to be placed on nodes with specific taints.

In the example below, the toleration is defined as jcnr=yes. This means that if a node is tainted with jcnr=yes or if it's not tainted at all, then the JCNR pods can be scheduled on that node. If a node is tainted with another key:value pair, then the JCNR pods cannot be scheduled on that node (depending on the effect).

note.p: This is a global setting.

row.entry: The key part of the key-value pair that represents the toleration.
Defines the match condition between the toleration and the taint. Valid values are Exists and Equal.
The value part of the key-value pair that represents the toleration. Field is present only if operator is Equal.
Defines the effect when the toleration does not match the taint. Valid values are NoExecute, NoSchedule, and PreferNoSchedule.

entry.p: (Optional) Enter a value for this parameter to override cRPD telemetry gRPC server default port of 50053.
If present, this indicates that you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.
This value should be a comma-delimited list of isolated CPU cores that you want to statically allocate to the forwarding plane (for example, cpu_core_mask: "2,3,22,23").
Comment this out if you want to use Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

note.p: You cannot use static CPU allocation and Kubernetes CPU Manager at the same time. Cloud-Native Router Readiness preflight checks, if enabled, will fail the installation if you specify both.

entry.p: If present, this indicates that you want to use the Kubernetes CPU Manager or Nokia CPU pooler (single-pod deployments only) to allocate CPU cores to the forwarding plane.

This value should be the number of guaranteed CPU cores that you want the Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate to the forwarding plane. You should set this value to at least one more than the number of forwarding cores.
Comment this out if you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

note.p: You cannot use static CPU allocation and Kubernetes CPU Manager at the same time. Using both can lead to unpredictable behavior.

entry.p: Specifies the CPU core(s) to allocate to vRouter DPDK control threads when using static CPU allocation. This list should be different than the cores listed in cpu_core_mask and can be the same as the list in serviceCoreMask.
Comment this out if you want to use Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

Specifies the CPU core(s) to allocate to vRouter DPDK service threads when using static CPU allocation. This list should be different than the cores listed in cpu_core_mask and can be the same as the list in dpdkCtrlThreadMask.
Comment this out if you want to use Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.
Specifies the number of CPU cores to allocate to vRouter DPDK service/control traffic when using the Kubernetes CPU Manager.
This number should be smaller than the number of guaranteedVrouterCpus cores. The remaining guaranteedVrouterCpus cores are allocated for forwarding.
Comment this out if you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

row.entry: The number of CPU cores (maximum 4) that you want Kubernetes CPU Manager to dedicate to your QoS schedulers. Comment this out if you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.
Set to true to restore the interfaces back to their original state in case the vRouter pod crashes or restarts or if Cloud-Native Router is uninstalled.

(Optional) Enable bond interface configurations for L3 mode deployments.
The bondInterfaceConfigs attribute and its child attributes are only applicable when running with pre-bound SR-IOV interfaces.
Comment out these attributes when running with non-pre-bound SR-IOV interfaces.
Information on the slave network interfaces (in name / namespace pairs) when using Network Attachment Definitions for L3 mode deployments. For an example on how to use this, see .

dlentry.dd: Name of the slave interface.
Namespace for the slave interface.

row.entry: Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) value for all physical interfaces (VFs and PFs). Default value is 9000.
Defines the QoS scheduler profiles that are referenced from the fabricInterface section.
The name of the QoS scheduler profile.

dlentry.dd: Specify the CPU core(s) to dedicate to the scheduler. If cpu_core_mask is specified, this should be a unique additional core(s).
Specify the bandwidth in Gbps.

entry.p: Pass any additional DPDK command line parameters. The --yield_option 0 is set by default and implies the DPDK forwarding cores will not yield their assigned CPU cores. Other common parameters that can be added are tx and rx descriptors and mempool. For example:

note.p: Changing the number of tx and rx descriptors and the mempool size affects the number of huge pages required. If you make explicit changes to these parameters, set the number of huge pages to 10 (x 1 GB).
See in for information on how to configure huge pages on a Wind River node and see for information on how to configure the number of huge pages that the Cloud-Native Router vRouter uses.

entry.p: (Optional) Indicates the global Dynamic Device Personalization (DDP) configuration. DDP provides datapath optimization at the NIC for traffic like GTPU, SCTP, etc. For a bond interface, all slave interface NICs must support DDP for the DDP configuration to be enabled. See for more details.
Options include auto, on, or off. Default is off.

note.p: The interface level ddp takes precedence over the global ddp configuration.

entry.p: (Optional) The TWAMP session reflector port (if you want TWAMP sessions to use vRouter timestamps). The vRouter listens to TWAMP test messages on this port and inserts/overwrites timestamps in TWAMP test messages. Timestamping TWAMP messages at the vRouter (instead of at cRPD) leads to more accurate measurements. Valid values are 862 and 49152 through 65535.
If this parameter is absent, then the vRouter does not insert or overwrite timestamps in the TWAMP session. Timestamps are taken and inserted by cRPD instead.
See .

row.entry: Set to false to enable the RPF check on all Cloud-Native Router fabric interfaces. By default, RPF check is disabled.

entry.p: (Optional) Configures cRPD telemetry settings. To learn more about telemetry, see .
Set to true to disable cRPD telemetry. Default is false, which means that cRPD telemetry is enabled by default.
The port that the cRPD telemetry exporter is listening to Prometheus queries on. Default is 8072.
One of warn, warning, info, debug, trace, or verbose. Default is info.
(Optional) Configures cRPD gNMI settings.

dlentry.dd: Set to true to enable the cRPD telemetry exporter to respond to gNMI requests.

entry.p: (Optional) Configures vRouter telemetry settings. To learn more about telemetry, see .

dlentry.dd: Specify the port that the vRouter telemetry exporter listens to Prometheus queries on. Default is 8070.
One of warn, warning, info, debug, trace, or verbose. Default is info.

(Optional) Configures vRouter gNMI settings.
enable - Set to true to enable the vRouter telemetry exporter to respond to gNMI requests.

row.entry: Set to true if you want Cloud-Native Router pod configuration to persist even after uninstallation. This option can only be set for L2 mode deployments. Default is false.

Set to true if you're using the cRPD CLI or NETCONF to configure JCNR. When set to true, the cRPD CLI and NETCONF configuration persists through node reboots, cRPD pod restarts, and Cloud-Native Router upgrades. Default is false.

entry.p: terminationGracePeriodSeconds

row.entry: Specify the timeout for the vrouter to clean up and unbind interfaces during uninstallation. By default, the timeout is 60 seconds. Extend this timeout for larger installations where JCNR is not uninstalling successfully.
Set to 1 to indicate a pre-bound SR-IOV interface. Default is 0.
Configures attributes related to the network attachment definitions.
Options are on or off. Default is off.
Specify the name of the network attachment definition.
Specify the namespace where the network attachment definition is created.
Specify fabric for a regular fabric interface or workload for an overlay management interface. Default is fabric.

entry.p: Set to trunk for L2 interfaces and do not configure for L3 interfaces. For example,
Provide a list of VLAN IDs associated with the trunk interface. Configure for L2 interfaces only.

Configure native-vlan-id with any of the VLAN IDs in the vlan-id-list to associate it with untagged data packets received on the physical interface of a trunk mode interface. Configure for L2 interfaces only. For example:
entry.codeblock: interface_mode: trunk vlan-id-list: [100, 200, 300, 700-705] storm-control-profile: rate_limit_pf1 native-vlan-id: 100
entry.p: See for more details.

row.entry: Configures network device resources for the network attachment definitions.
Set the limit for the number of SR-IOV interfaces used for each network attachment definition.
Set the requested number of SR-IOV interfaces for each network attachment definition.
Set to true to expose services for agent introspect, vrouter telemetry and crpd telemetry
Can be set to NodePort, ClusterIP, and LoadBalancer.
Configure a valid IP address to expose the ClusterIP service type.
Configure a routable IP address to expose the service for external access.
Configure an IP for LoadBalancer type service if supported by the cloud provider.
Configure this section if the service type is NodePort.
Specify the port number to expose the crpd Prometheus-based telemetry service.
Specify the port number to expose the vRouter Prometheus-based telemetry service.
Specify the port number to expose the agent Introspect service.
Specify the port number to expose the crpd gNMI-based telemetry service.
Specify the port number to expose the gNMI-based telemetry service.

section.title: Helm Chart Description for Azure Deployment
section.p: Customize the Helm chart using the Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_<release>/helmchart/jcnr/values.yaml file. We provide a copy of the default values.yaml in .
contains a description of the configurable attributes in values.yaml for an Azure deployment.

table.title: Helm Chart Description for Azure Deployment

row.entry: Set to true to install syslog-ng.

Defines the Docker registry for the Cloud-Native Router container images. The default value is enterprise-hub.juniper.net. The images provided in the tarball are tagged with the default registry name. If you choose to host the container images to a private registry, replace the default value with your registry URL.

(Optional) Defines the repository path for the Cloud-Native Router container images. This is a global key that takes precedence over the repository paths under the common section. Default is jcnr-container-prod/.
(Optional) Defines the Docker registry authentication credentials. You can configure credentials to either the Juniper Networks enterprise-hub.juniper.net registry or your private registry.
Base64 representation of your Docker registry credentials. See for more information.
Name of the secret object that will be created.
Deploys contrail-tools container that provides a centralized location for all vRouter tools and CLI commands.
Set to true to install the contrail-tools container.
Defines repository paths and tags for the Cloud-Native Router container images. Use defaults unless using a private registry.
Defines the repository path. The default value is jcnr-container-prod/. The global repository key takes precedence if defined.
Defines the image tag. The default value is configured to the appropriate tag number for the Cloud-Native Router release version.

entry.p: Set to true to enable Cloud-Native Router Readiness preflight and postflight checks during installation. Comment this out or set to false to disable Cloud-Native Router Readiness preflight and postflight checks.
Preflight checks verify that your infrastructure can support JCNR. Preflight checks take place before Cloud-Native Router is installed.
Postflight checks verify that your Cloud-Native Router installation is working properly. Postflight checks take place after Cloud-Native Router is installed.
See .

row.entry: (Optional) Indicates the number of replicas for cRPD. Default is 1. The value for this key must be specified for multi-node clusters. The value is equal to the number of nodes running JCNR.

entry.p: Provide a list of interfaces to be bound to the DPDK.

note.p: Use the L3 only section to configure fabric interfaces for Azure. The L2 only and L2-L3 sections are not applicable for Azure deployments. Do not configure interface_mode for any of the interfaces.

entry.p: For example:
entry.codeblock: # L3 only - eth1: ddp: "off" - eth2: ddp: "off"
entry.p: See for more information.
Specifies the QoS scheduler profile applicable to this interface. See the qosSchedulerProfiles section.
If you don't specify a profile, then the QoS scheduler is disabled for this interface, which means that packets are scheduled with no regard to traffic class.

row.entry:
Defines the log severity. Available value options are: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, and ERR.
Leave it set to the default INFO unless instructed to change it by Juniper Networks support.

entry.p: The defined directory stores various JCNR-related descriptive logs such as contrail-vrouter-agent.log, contrail-vrouter-dpdk.log, etc. Default is /var/log/jcnr/.
Indicates the absolute path to the file that stores syslog-ng generated notifications in JSON format. Default is /var/log/jcnr/jcnr_notifications.json.
Indicates the core_pattern for the core file. If left blank, then Cloud-Native Router pods will not overwrite the default pattern on the host.

note.p: Set the core_pattern on the host before deploying JCNR. You can change the value in /etc/sysctl.conf. For example, kernel.core_pattern=/var/crash/core_%e_%p_%i_%s_%h_%t.gz

row.entry: Indicates the path to the core file. Default is /var/crash.

entry.p: (Optional) Defines labels on nodes to determine where to place the vRouter pods.
By default the vRouter pods are deployed to all nodes of a cluster.
In the example below, the node affinity label is defined as key1=jcnr. You must apply this label to each node where Cloud-Native Router is to be deployed:
entry.codeblock: nodeAffinity: - key: key1 operator: In values: - jcnr

note.p: This key is a global setting.

row.entry: Key-value pair that represents a node label that must be matched to apply the node affinity.
Defines the relationship between the node label and the set of values in the matchExpression parameters in the pod specification. This value can be In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Lt, or Gt.

entry.p: (Optional) Defines tolerations to allow JCNR pods to be placed on nodes with specific taints.

In the example below, the toleration is defined as jcnr=yes. This means that if a node is tainted with jcnr=yes or if it's not tainted at all, then the JCNR pods can be scheduled on that node. If a node is tainted with another key:value pair, then the JCNR pods cannot be scheduled on that node (depending on the effect).

note.p: This is a global setting.

row.entry: The key part of the key-value pair that represents the toleration.
Defines the match condition between the toleration and the taint. Valid values are Exists and Equal.
The value part of the key-value pair that represents the toleration. Field is present only if operator is Equal.
Defines the effect when the toleration does not match the taint. Valid values are NoExecute, NoSchedule, and PreferNoSchedule.
(Optional) The default path is /opt/cni/bin. You can override the default path with the path in your distribution (for example, /var/opt/cni/bin).

entry.p: (Optional) Enter a value for this parameter to override cRPD telemetry gRPC server default port of 50053.
If present, this indicates that you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

This value should be a comma-delimited list of isolated CPU cores that you want to statically allocate to the forwarding plane (for example, cpu_core_mask: "2,3,22,23"). Use the cores not used by the host OS.
Comment this out if you want to use Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

note.p: You cannot use static CPU allocation and Kubernetes CPU Manager at the same time. Cloud-Native Router Readiness preflight checks, if enabled, will fail the installation if you specify both.

entry.p: If present, this indicates that you want to use the Kubernetes CPU Manager or Nokia CPU pooler (single-pod deployments only) to allocate CPU cores to the forwarding plane.

This value should be the number of guaranteed CPU cores that you want the Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate to the forwarding plane. You should set this value to at least one more than the number of forwarding cores.
Comment this out if you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

note.p: You cannot use static CPU allocation and Kubernetes CPU Manager at the same time. Using both can lead to unpredictable behavior.

entry.p: Specifies the CPU core(s) to allocate to vRouter DPDK control threads when using static CPU allocation. This list should be different than the cores listed in cpu_core_mask and can be the same as the list in serviceCoreMask.
Comment this out if you want to use Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

Specifies the CPU core(s) to allocate to vRouter DPDK service threads when using static CPU allocation. This list should be different than the cores listed in cpu_core_mask and can be the same as the list in dpdkCtrlThreadMask.
Comment this out if you want to use Kubernetes CPU Manager to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.
Specifies the number of CPU cores to allocate to vRouter DPDK service/control traffic when using the Kubernetes CPU Manager.
This number should be smaller than the number of guaranteedVrouterCpus cores. The remaining guaranteedVrouterCpus cores are allocated for forwarding.
Comment this out if you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.

row.entry: The number of CPU cores (maximum 4) that you want Kubernetes CPU Manager to dedicate to your QoS schedulers. Comment this out if you want to use static CPU allocation to allocate cores to the forwarding plane.
Set to true to restore the interfaces back to their original state in case the vRouter pod crashes or restarts or if Cloud-Native Router is uninstalled.
Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) value for all physical interfaces (VFs and PFs). Default is 9000.
Defines the QoS scheduler profiles that are referenced from the fabricInterface section.
The name of the QoS scheduler profile.

dlentry.dd: Specify the CPU core(s) to dedicate to the scheduler. If cpu_core_mask is specified, this should be a unique additional core(s).
Specify the bandwidth in Gbps.

entry.p: Pass any additional DPDK command line parameters. The --yield_option 0 is set by default and implies the DPDK forwarding cores will not yield their assigned CPU cores. Other common parameters that can be added are tx and rx descriptors and mempool. For example:

note.p: Changing the number of tx and rx descriptors and the mempool size affects the number of huge pages required. If you make explicit changes to these parameters, set the number of huge pages to 10 (x 1 GB).
See for information on how to configure huge pages.

entry.p: (Optional) The TWAMP session reflector port (if you want TWAMP sessions to use vRouter timestamps). The vRouter listens to TWAMP test messages on this port and inserts/overwrites timestamps in TWAMP test messages. Timestamping TWAMP messages at the vRouter (instead of at cRPD) leads to more accurate measurements. Valid values are 862 and 49152 through 65535.
If this parameter is absent, then the vRouter does not insert or overwrite timestamps in the TWAMP session. Timestamps are taken and inserted by cRPD instead.
See .
Set to dpdk.

row.entry: Set to false to enable the RPF check on all Cloud-Native Router fabric interfaces. By default, RPF check is disabled.

entry.p: (Optional) Configures cRPD telemetry settings. To learn more about telemetry, see .
Set to true to disable cRPD telemetry. Default is false, which means that cRPD telemetry is enabled by default.
The port that the cRPD telemetry exporter is listening to Prometheus queries on. Default is 8072.
One of warn, warning, info, debug, trace, or verbose. Default is info.
(Optional) Configures cRPD gNMI settings.

dlentry.dd: Set to true to enable the cRPD telemetry exporter to respond to gNMI requests.

entry.p: (Optional) Configures vRouter telemetry settings. To learn more about telemetry, see .

dlentry.dd: Specify the port that the vRouter telemetry exporter listens to Prometheus queries on. Default is 8070.
One of warn, warning, info, debug, trace, or verbose. Default is info.

(Optional) Configures vRouter gNMI settings.
enable - Set to true to enable the vRouter telemetry exporter to respond to gNMI requests.

row.entry: Set to true if you want Cloud-Native Router pod configuration to persist even after uninstallation. This option can only be set for L2 mode deployments. Default is false.

Set to true if you're using the cRPD CLI or NETCONF to configure JCNR. When set to true, the cRPD CLI and NETCONF configuration persists through node reboots, cRPD pod restarts, and Cloud-Native Router upgrades. Default is false.

entry.p: terminationGracePeriodSeconds

row.entry: Specify the timeout for the vrouter to clean up and unbind interfaces during uninstallation. By default, the timeout is 60 seconds. Extend this timeout for larger installations where JCNR is not uninstalling successfully.
Set to true to expose services for agent introspect, vrouter telemetry and crpd telemetry
Can be set to NodePort, ClusterIP, and LoadBalancer.
Configure a valid IP address to expose the ClusterIP service type.
Configure a routable IP address to expose the service for external access.
Configure an IP for LoadBalancer type service if supported by the cloud provider.
Configure this section if the service type is NodePort.
Specify the port number to expose the crpd Prometheus-based telemetry service.
Specify the port number to expose the vRouter Prometheus-based telemetry service.
Specify the port number to expose the agent Introspect service.
Specify the port number to expose the crpd gNMI-based telemetry service.
Specify the port number to expose the gNMI-based telemetry service.
